title: Hot cross buns
subtitle: Spiced Easter buns with currants, a piped cross, and a sugar syrup glaze
excerpt: Twelve spiced, enriched buns with currants and mixed peel, a white cross piped in flour paste before baking, and a glossy sugar syrup glaze applied hot from the oven. The cross sets pale against the dark glazed surface. Eat warm, split and buttered.
sourceNotes: Hot cross buns are documented in English baking tradition from at least the seventeenth century, with the spiced enriched bun sold on Good Friday well established by the eighteenth century. Hannah Glasse, The Art of Cookery Made Plain and Easy (1747), Project Gutenberg: documents spiced enriched buns with dried fruit glazed with sugar syrup, noting the cross cut or piped on the surface before baking. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: the hot cross bun section documents a mixed-spice and currant enriched bun with a flour paste cross piped or cut before baking, and a boiled sugar syrup glaze brushed on immediately after.

recipe.freezeNotes: Cool completely and freeze in a bag. Split and toast from frozen.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Shape the buns, arrange on the tray, cover and refrigerate overnight. Bring to room temperature for 30 minutes, then pipe the cross and bake.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, middle shelf. The enriched, sugared buns colour fast — reduce to 190°C if they are browning too quickly before the 15-minute mark

glossaryTerms.definition: The cross piped onto hot cross buns with a thick flour-and-water paste just before baking. The paste is thick enough to hold its shape during the prove and bake — too thin and it spreads flat and disappears. It sets in the oven, producing a raised, pale cross against the dark glazed surface of the finished bun.

content.text: Hot cross buns need an enriched, slightly sticky dough: soft enough that the buns stay tender after baking, firm enough that they hold their round shape through the second prove. The spice mix is important — mixed spice alone is fine, but a little extra cinnamon deepens the flavour. The cross is not optional, but you can cut it rather than pipe it if you have no piping bag: run a sharp knife across the top of each bun just before it goes in the oven.

Combine the {{strong-bread-flour}}, {{yeast-fast-action}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, {{caster-sugar}} for the dough, {{mixed-spice}}, and {{cinnamon-ground}} in a large bowl. Beat the {{eggs}} with the {{whole-milk}} and pour in. Mix to a rough dough, then knead for 5 minutes. Add the {{unsalted-butter}} in three pieces, working each in before adding the next. Knead for 5 more minutes until smooth and elastic. The dough will be soft. Press in the {{dried-currants}} and {{mixed-peel}} and knead briefly to distribute evenly.
Cover and leave in a warm place for 90 minutes until roughly doubled.

Line a baking tray with baking paper. Divide the dough into 12 equal pieces (about 90 g each). Shape each into a tight ball by folding the edges into the centre, turning seam-side down, and rolling firmly under a cupped hand. Arrange in a 3×4 grid on the tray with 2 cm between each.
Cover loosely and leave for 60 minutes until puffed and almost touching. Preheat the oven to 200°C fan.
Pipe the cross and bake, 20–22 minutes

Mix the {{plain-flour}} and the {{water}} for the cross into a thick, smooth paste. Spoon into a piping bag and snip a small hole. Pipe a continuous line across each row of buns, then across each column, to form the

. Bake for 20–22 minutes until deep brown and firm. While they bake, dissolve the {{caster-sugar}} for the glaze in the {{water}} for the glaze in a small saucepan over medium heat, bring to a simmer for 2 minutes, then remove. As soon as the buns come out of the oven, brush the glaze over the hot surface. Leave on the tray for 10 minutes before moving to a rack.
